title: The Flinch
story_reference: Based on story-002-the-rope-and-the-snake.md, scene: the disciple sees the snake in the dark and grabs the Guru's arm
hook: He saw a snake in the dark. His body believed it before his mind could speak. His heart was already racing.

script.visual: Dusk. A narrow forest path. Shot from low, from the ground. Copper light bleeding through trees. Two sets of feet walking.
script.voiceover: They were walking along the river at dusk. The sun was going down.

script.visual: Follow shot of the two walking. The Guru a few steps ahead, white cloth catching the last light. The path narrows. Shadows pool between roots.
script.voiceover: The old man moved the way men move who have walked a path ten thousand times. The disciple followed. His mind was still loud.

script.visual: Abrupt shift in sound — a single low note, almost subsonic. The camera sees, before the disciple does, something coiled in the leaves. Thick-bodied. Patterned. Motionless.
Extreme close-up: the coil. Then the disciple's face — a full-body flinch. Heart pounding. He grabs the Guru's arm, hard. His breathing goes ragged.
script.voiceover: His body reacted before his mind could. Heart slamming. Breath catching. Feet stumbling back.

script.visual: The disciple points, hand shaking. 'Stop — there. A snake.' The Guru does not flinch. The Guru looks. Long beat.
Without a word, the Guru walks forward, bends down, picks it up. The camera sees what the disciple now sees. It is a rope. Frayed. Old. Someone tied a boat to a tree upstream once.
script.voiceover: The old man did not flinch. He walked forward. He bent down. He picked it up.

script.visual: Close on the disciple's face. The adrenaline drains. His hands are still shaking. He exhales, hollow and foolish. The Guru holds the rope, quiet.
script.voiceover: The Guru said: 'Feel your heart. It is still racing, yes?' The disciple nodded. 'From what?'

script.visual: Hold on the rope in the Guru's open palm. Black frame. One word.
script.voiceover: 'There was no snake. There was never a snake.'

cta: Scene three. The body was terrified. The mind was certain. Nothing that caused the fear existed.
caption:
He saw a snake. His heart raced. His hands shook. Adrenaline flooded his body.

It was a rope.

The Vivekachudamani uses this image to explain Māyā — the force that makes the nervous system react, completely, to something that was never there.

How much of what you are afraid of right now falls into the same category?

māyā
music_suggestion: Diegetic only at the start — footsteps, distant water, forest ambience. A single sub-bass note enters at 15s, just below audibility, and sits there for 15 seconds. When the Guru picks it up, the note cuts — dead silence — for four seconds. Then a single sustained cello enters, unresolved, for the closing image.
casting_notes: Two characters: The Seeker (mid-30s to 40s, modern-dressed in simple linen now, no longer corporate — he has been at the ashram a week) and The Guru (older, economy of movement, does not startle). The scene needs a real rope — coiled, frayed, about the thickness of a pond snake — and shooting at actual dusk, not simulated. If actors unavailable: shoot in first-person POV, hands only, with footsteps and breath as the primary soundtrack.
